military.reason: Military facet covers distinct sub-domains each with substantial scholarly literature and concepts: wars/campaigns, organization/units, tactics/technology, command/strategy, civil conflicts, and naval warfare

sub_scopes.scope_description: Major wars, battles, and military campaigns of the Republic including Punic, Macedonian, Gallic, and Social Wars
sub_scopes.needs: Chronological sequencing, battle geography, commander prosopography, casualty analysis

sub_scopes.scope_description: Legion structure, unit types, recruitment, and military reforms throughout Republican period
sub_scopes.needs: Unit hierarchies, equipment catalogs, recruitment demographics, reform timelines

sub_scopes.scope_description: Internal military conflicts from Gracchi through Augustus including Sulla, Marius, Caesar
sub_scopes.needs: Factional analysis, veteran settlement patterns, constitutional impacts

sub_scopes.scope_description: Specific battles and military engagements with tactical and strategic significance
sub_scopes.needs: Tactical analysis, terrain studies, casualty records, source criticism

sub_scopes.scope_description: Military leadership, command structures, strategic planning, and general competency
sub_scopes.needs: Prosopographical data, command hierarchies, strategic doctrines, leadership analysis

cross_facet_links.label: Pyrrhic War
cross_facet_links.evidence: This war demonstrates Roman military adaptation against Hellenistic warfare tactics and represents early Republican expansion
cross_facet_links.reasoning: Wars necessarily involve diplomatic aspects - alliances, treaties, negotiations with Greek states

verdicts.label: Punic Wars
verdicts.evidence: Core Republican military conflicts extensively referenced in Polybius commentary and civil war contexts

verdicts.label: Pyrrhic War
verdicts.evidence: Important early Republican military engagement showing adaptation to new warfare styles

verdicts.label: Macedonian Wars
verdicts.evidence: Key Republican expansion wars in Eastern Mediterranean, well-documented in classical sources

verdicts.label: Social War of 91-87 BCE
verdicts.evidence: Critical military conflict that preceded civil wars and demonstrated military recruitment issues

verdicts.label: Gallic War
verdicts.evidence: Major Republican conquest campaign with extensive documentation and military significance

verdicts.label: Praetorian Guard
verdicts.evidence: Primarily Imperial institution; Republican bodyguard arrangements were different and less formalized

verdicts.label: Fimbrian legions
verdicts.evidence: Specific Republican military units involved in civil wars, showing evolution of professional army

verdicts.label: empire
verdicts.evidence: Too broad and conceptual for Military facet; belongs in Political discussions of Republican expansion and governance

all_insights.label: Roman legion
all_insights.evidence: Polybius commentary discusses legion organization extensively; candidates include Fimbrian legions (Q60792542) showing specific legion types were significant
all_insights.reasoning: Core military unit missing from candidates but fundamental to all Republican warfare and military organization

all_insights.label: Marian reforms
all_insights.evidence: Lange's work on militarization discusses major military reforms; civil war context shows importance of professional army transformation
all_insights.reasoning: Critical transformation in Republican military history that professionalized the army and contributed to civil wars

all_insights.label: Pyrrhic War
all_insights.evidence: This war demonstrates Roman military adaptation against Hellenistic warfare tactics and represents early Republican expansion
all_insights.reasoning: Wars necessarily involve diplomatic aspects - alliances, treaties, negotiations with Greek states

all_insights.label: Praetorian Guard
all_insights.evidence: Listed as Republican candidate but Praetorian Guard was primarily Imperial institution; Republican equivalents were different bodyguard arrangements
all_insights.reasoning: Need to clarify Republican vs Imperial military institutions to avoid anachronistic concepts
